speaker: interviewer
text: We are now continuing on tape number four, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four, you're correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. The interview with survivor Jack Sandbrand. Today's date is January 22, 1995. We're in Rancho Bernardo, San Diego, California. Jack, we were talking about the war, and you have surrounded this German general. And now the offensive began. And you were in the mortar unit. What did you do in the mortar unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a forward-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Spotter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spotter, yeah. I would call it a spotter. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to carry telephone lines-- spools, big spools with telephone wire-- which we hung up with a stick on different-- or we also had a radio contact. One of the men carried a radio. And we contacted the rear, and we used to tell them so many feet to the left or so many yards to the right. We spotted where to fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: You directed fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Directed the fire, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in doing that, did you stay in one place or did you move around?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never stood more than 24 hours in one place. The whole unit, the whole division was mobile. It was 120-millimeter mortars that were towed with a Studebaker lend-lease truck, American lend-lease. And we will spend-- we will come to a point where we get set up, open fire within an hour after being set up, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hit hard a certain area for two, three hours, and when they started firing back at us, we'd pack up and move on to a different area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you hit and ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Though we got hit a few times. We didn't run quick enough. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In the process of moving through on the-- on the offensive, did you have the opportunity to encounter any Jewish communities?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was mostly wild country. We were not in cities. It was farms. We were moving up through country roads full of mud. [LAUGHS] It was-- this wasn't a city where you go in-- you go into Berlin or Warsaw [LAUGHS] or-- this was mainly collective farms that we were attacking. Well, we weren't attacking the collective farms. We were attacking the Germans who were there-- the Nazis. And we were overrun-- overrun them. As they were retreating, we were advancing the same. Once in a while, we came through a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you happen to have any-- to see what-- I'm sorry. Did you happen to capture any Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends who caught them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We used to see many atrocities on the roads, on the small roads in the villages when we liberated some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of atrocities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dead women and children [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] just laying in the-- in the-- frozen to death. People were killed for no reason. And the ones that survived explained to us what happened. Some of them were taken out to be drained of blood-- at least, this is what they told us-- to get the blood for the German soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We found a Russian lieutenant that they interrogated. What they did is they took off his boots, and they put his feet in a pail of water, and his feet were frozen. He was frozen to death like that outside. That I call an atrocity. We found areas where a group of women nurses in their uniforms was slaughtered with the doctors. And they had definitely had a Red Cross sign there.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Ger-- by who else?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of these killed because they were Jewish or because they were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I couldn't tell you. I don't know. There might have been some-- some Jewish nurses or doctors, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did the Russian military respond to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They responded the same way. We caught some Germans. If they were SS, we didn't hold on to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? Shoot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We shot them. Some of them were hung. It depends. Some of them were brought in, interrogated. And the captain used to say, shoot the son of a bitch. [LAUGHS] Look, it was war, and there were atrocities. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was only once in a case where I had to shoot somebody. I mean, seeing his face, the captain ordered me to do it. But otherwise, I try to stay away from it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] though I hated them with all my being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have the occasion to see any concentration camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We liberated many a town where they supposed to have been ghettos. In some areas where we came in, men came in from the woods with guns. They were partisans. And they told us what was transpired. Some of the women came out from the woods. I couldn't tell you exactly where it happened. But as we were advancing west, we kept running into these people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the partisans Jewish that you know about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them identified themselves as Jews. They were proud of being a Jew. One of them told me that the commander of his outfit was a Jew, of the partisan outfit. But exactly where or when, I couldn't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because I was in a regular unit outfit that was disciplined. We traveled a certain way. We ate a certain way. [LAUGHS] You couldn't break away. You were just part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. Well, when you came into a community, when you passed through a community--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay long enough in a community until after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, during the war, do you know if the Russian military-- not your unit, but the military as a whole-- did anything to help the civilian population that you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they-- whatever food they had, they tried to feed these people. Some of them were sent to hospitals. To-- they called in doctors. I would say yes. Our unit didn't stay long enough in one spot. We just didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went from community to community to community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And through the communities very quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quickly, except in Praga Varsava. Praga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Praga. How do you spell Praga?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish, you spell, P-R-A-G-A, Praga.

speaker: interviewer
text: Praga. And tell me about Praga Varsava.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for, I think, three and a half to four months. This was after we liberated Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were dug in a block from the Vistula River, Wisla. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the other side of the river were the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So where we were dug in-- well, some of the men were dug in, the infantry, right at the edge of the river. Then they had apartment buildings. And we were right behind the apartment buildings set up with our mortars. And the point people went into the apartment buildings, high up, to observe the German side with their binoculars.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't cross the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't cross the river. We had orders not to cross. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But some people were trying to cross from the enemy side, escape. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we had standing orders not to let anybody cross from the enemy's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you shoot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shoot them. The infantry were shooting them. No matter who they were, they didn't want them for some reason. The way I see it is that they had the Polish-- after they eliminated all the Jews, the Polish underground-- who was also responsible for some of that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called themselves Armia Krajowa, the People's Army. And they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started the fight against the-- the Germans. The Russians didn't want to interfere. They wanted that army exterminated by the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because they were planning ahead to take the country over, you know, to-- so Poland would become a communist country. So as many Poles from this underground Polish army that died, that was their-- this is the way I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Well, let's go back just a little bit. You said that you liberated Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about coming into Treblinka? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the point man with two other car-- Jeeps, like. I don't know if they were American Jeeps or what. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the second Jeep to drive in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw stacks of bodies [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] piled. [SIGHS] Hundreds of bodies, frozen, [SOBBING] piled up on top of the other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] shacks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were full of bodies, and some emaciated living skeletons, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that when they saw us come out, in the Russian uniform, come in, they started coming out like shadows walking. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's all we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Either August or September, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Some of them spoke Jewish. Some of them spoke Polish. The-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and as we drove in, we saw some of the guards were still shooting prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we opened fire, so they gave up. And we caught about a dozen of them or so in Russian uniforms-- in German uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And half of them spoke Russian, Ukrainian. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And right behind us, the main unit came in with our colonel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we came over and we told them that these people spoke Ukrainian, he says, hang the son of a bitches. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So 12 men ran over, grabbed each one of them, and they hung them on the lamp posts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no questions asked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There were women there speaking Ukrainian, and they were part of the Ukrainian group that collaborated with the-- with the Nazis. They claimed they were prisoners, but they were dressed in-- in nice civilian clothes. They weren't undernourished. You know-- [LAUGHS] you know what I'm saying? What they did with them, I don't know. Did you have a chance to speak with any of the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to two of them, yes. I don't remember their names. I was there another two hours, maybe. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man-- one man told me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was from a town somewhere near Warsaw, and he was in a ghetto. And he was brought in to be exterminated. They worked them to death. And then when they were no more of use, that was it. They were shooting them. They were hanging them. They were-- look-- gassing them. The ovens were going day and night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they just didn't have enough time to burn the bodies that were stacked up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the ovens?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw some of them, yes, with the ashes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an idea. I knew they weren't baking bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By that time, it was already 1944. We knew already what they were capable of, what they were doing. The lectures-- in between fights, we had lectures by political commissars that were enlightening the troops what was going on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we cared about-- much about these political commissars, you know what I'm saying? It was a bore. Many of their conversations was boring, because they were selling us the communist line. But we knew how to separate one from the other. We were not children anymore. Do you know if the Soviet army did anything to help the prisoners in Treblinka, particularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say yes, yeah, though I wasn't there that long. But I would say, they didn't let them starve. Unless they gave them too much food, which I doubt, and that killed them. Like I heard in Bergen-Belsen, 5,000 died after liberation because they gave them too much food.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Where did you go from Treblinka and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we advanced all the way to Praga.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed there for four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, on January 8th or 10th or 11th, I'm not sure which exact date, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they dismantled all the-- the mortars. We hooked up. We were on the move again. We heard firing. Artillery opened up early in the morning. The sky lit up at 4:00 in the morning like it was daylight. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Russian planes were bombing the other side. They were softening them up. Katushas-- I don't know if you know what katushas are-- were firing by the thousands, these rockets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we waited from about 4:00 or 5:00 in the morning till 7:00. And we were on the move. Apparently the infantry broke through to the other side. The Wisla River was frozen solid, like a rock. The trucks went right across. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came into Warsaw. And Warsaw, there was nothing left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was rubble, the whole city. I didn't see-- as we were driving through Warsaw, I didn't see one standing building that wasn't damaged or destroyed. And we didn't stay in Warsaw, either. We drove right through it. And we kept advancing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came to where the Germans tried to hold on. We opened fire for a few hours, and we moved on again. They-- we broke through again. And each week, we broke through again. And each two weeks, we broke through again. And we got to-- to this place where we found-- where-- were I found these Hungarian women in a-- in this Hungarian camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see much of the camp, either. We drove in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I see the ditch beside the road. And we see bodies moving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I stopped the Jeep, and I walked over. And I hear them speak in Yiddish, the women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they spoke Hungarian, which I didn't understand. So I didn't know who they were, but I realized they were Jewish. So I said to her, [YIDDISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all I had to say. And she jumped up, one of them, and grabbed me around the-- my feet. Please, help my sister-- help my schwester. [YIDDISH]. Help my schwester. [YIDDISH] She wasn't worried about herself, a younger sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So my driver and I, we picked him up and we put them in the back of the Jeep. And we drove back to the city, which was about five kilometers, Poznan. This was in a field, mind you, a country road.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knocked on the door, and they opened up the door. And we got inside, and we threw them out. And we put the girls in-- into the beds, covered them with the [NON-ENGLISH], [LAUGHS] you know, the covers, whatever, made out of-- it's not like blankets we have here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like down? Like feathers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Down, feathers, or whatever. I couldn't stay. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was talking to them for about a half an hour. And we called the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose doctor did you call?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Russian. Our-- our doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Military doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Dr. Levine.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish doctor, yes. Captain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he came over within an hour. He couldn't-- he had wounded people to attend to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the way I understand, he took care of them and he sent them to a hospital. But before we left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sister that begged me to help her, and her sister and cousin, gave me a piece of paper with her brother's name and address in Tel Aviv. And she says, [YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: In English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Translate that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Please [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] write to my brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he is in Tel Aviv-- that we are alive. And that was the end of the conversation. I got into my Jeep, and we drove away. I put that piece of paper in my pocket. I said to myself, in this war, we kept advancing and moving. When will I have time to write a letter? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We drove and we reached the German border. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we stopped for three days. You ask me where, I don't remember. I think it was on the Oder River. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I-- we had three days. I washed myself up. I was filthy. I didn't have any change of underwear. [LAUGHS] I had lice, which the only way to get rid of, they kept spraying us with a chemical powder. We didn't have time. But we were near the river, so I got in and I washed myself up, and put on the same old dirty clothes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I got into my pocket, and I find that piece of paper. So I wrote a letter, telling him that I helped his sisters and cousin. I remember their names at that time. I don't remember their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you write the letter in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote in Russian. Apparently, he knew where to translate it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And never in a lifetime-- and I wrote my return address, [NON-ENGLISH], Field Post so-and-so and so-and-so, and my name, [? Yaakov ?] Sandbrand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He received it, and I get a telegram. And I still have that telegram. It came to my field post. And he says, thank you for helping my relatives, or sisters, or cousins. And what happened to my parents? What did I know what happened to his parents? Jack, eventually the war ended.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how did you get out of the Russian army? How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before I tell you that, is he also wrote-- the brother of these girls-- wrote a letter to his uncle in Brooklyn, who lived in Fort Hamilton Parkway, Mr. And Mrs. Weiss. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before the war was over, I get a letter. Within a week after-- a week or two weeks after I got the telegram, I get the letter, addressed to my field post, written in Yiddish, from Mr and Mrs. Weiss. And it says, thank you for helping my nieces. All three of them were his nieces. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he asked me, also, what happened to his sister and brother-in-law-- you know, the parents. And he says to me, thank you for helping them. A mountain never gets together with a mountain, but you never can tell about a person, getting together with a person. He says, anything you need or want, just let me know, and I'll try to help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I answer them right away, a letter. And I said to him, I don't want anything. We're still in the war right now, and I don't see where you can do anything. But I have aunts and uncles who live in the United States. Find them, and tell them that I'm alive. We're going to pause here to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Jack.
